body.p:
With XSLTConceptsInheritance , constructs from other programming paradigms can be replicated, for example inheritance. In a specialization, an already existing implementation is adopted and extended or restricted.
UWE test footnote (20/40, callout T): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
UWE supplement (fn-add) for the footnote index.
The advantage is that you do not have to rewrite everything from scratch. This reduces redundancy, leads to better maintainability, and lower error-proneness.

section.title: Example: Parameterization
section.p: Typically, you implement a stylesheet for a specific output format and a product variant. Additional variants and formats are then added step by step.
Ideally, a complete specification would be available at the start of the implementation... But of course, that is rarely the case.
For this reason, it is important to think of a good strategy so that the architecture does not degenerate into spaghetti code.
A good option would be to leverage the XSLT import precedence, cf. chapter Uniqueness of the Rule Base.
If you want to introduce additional parameters at a later point, a switch like the following would have to be updated in multiple places in the code.
section.pre: <xsl:choose> <xsl:when test="$myParameter='this_option'"> <!-- do this --> </xsl:when> <xsl:when test="$myParameter='that_option'"> <!-- do that --> </xsl:when> [...] </xsl:choose>
section.p: It is better to maintain a core stylesheetParameterizationCore Stylesheet that is well-tested for one format and one product variantFormat and Product Variants. This core stylesheet is then simply imported for a new variant and relevant parts are overridden for the new "specialization"ConceptsGeneralization and Specialization . For example, a rule for setting the header on each page might be implemented as follows:
section.pre: <xsl:template name="render-header"> <!-- print logo on the left side spanning two rows--> <!-- print some metadata right side first row --> <!-- print a running header right side second row --> </xsl:template>
section.p: If in a new format, e.g. A5, this logic is swapped out and only a single line is printed, e.g. because there is not much space, then in a "derived" stylesheet the rule would simply be reimplemented.ParameterizationSub-Stylesheet
section.pre: <xsl:choose> <xsl:template name="render-header"> <!-- print a running header on left side --> <!-- print logo on right side --> </xsl:template>
section.p: This template now takes precedence and is used for evaluation, with the consequence that the header is printed as a single line only. The nice thing about these "named templates"ConceptsNamed Template is that they can also be used within variables:
The template get-margin-width can be overridden in a "sub" stylesheet without having to modify the variable accesses in the core stylesheet. An assignment like:
would not need to be touched anywhere else in the code, which is of course very convenient.

section.title: Example: Specialized Case Distinction
section.p: ConceptsSpecialized Case Distinction If an output format is added at a later point, you can use inheritance to cleverly distinguish between output formats. An existing template "cover":
section.pre: <xsl:template match="cover"> <fo:page-sequence master-reference="coverFront" force-page-count="auto"> <fo:flow flow-name="xsl-region-body"> [...] <fo:page-sequence master-reference="coverBack" force-page-count="auto"> <fo:flow flow-name="xsl-region-body"> [...]
section.p: can be split into a template for a front and back cover:
section.pre:
<xsl:template match="cover"> <fo:page-sequence master-reference="coverFront" force-page-count="auto"> <fo:flow flow-name="xsl-region-body"> [...] </xsl:template>
<xsl:template name="cover-back-switch"> <xsl:call-template name="cover-back"/> </xsl:template>
<xsl:template name="cover-back"> <fo:page-sequence master-reference="coverBack" force-page-count="auto"> <fo:flow flow-name="xsl-region-body"> [...]
section.p: The template "cover-back-switch" can then be overridden in an output format to, for example, toggle the back cover conditionally.

Design PatternRoughly structuring an algorithm in a superclass and refining details in subclasses is a common design pattern in object-oriented programming. This methodology can also be beautifully realized using named templates in XSLT. It is no coincidence that the corresponding OO pattern is called Template Method Wikipedia page on the Template Method Pattern
